A.name: Alligator
A.size: Length: 10 to 15 feet (3 to 4.5 meters); Weight: 450 to 1,000 pounds (200 to 450 kg)
A.favorite_food: Fish and small animals living near the water
A.fun_fact: An alligator's powerful jaws can snap shut with incredible strength, but the muscles that open their mouth are quite weak—a human can easily hold an alligator's mouth closed with their hands!

A.name: Ant
A.native_to: Found all over the world (except Antarctica)
A.size: Length: 0.08 to 1 inch (2 to 25 mm); Weight: A tiny fraction of an ounce
A.favorite_food: Sweet things like sugar and nectar
A.fun_fact: Ants are true powerhouses; they can lift objects that weigh up to 50 times their own body weight!

A.name: Antelope
A.size: Height: 1 to 6 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 15 to 2,000 pounds
A.favorite_food: Fresh grass, leaves, and tender shoots
A.fun_fact: Unlike deer that shed their antlers every year, antelopes have permanent horns made of bone covered in a sheath of keratin.

A.name: Ape
A.size: Height: 2.5 to 5.5 feet tall; Weight: 30 to 400 pounds
A.favorite_food: Ripe tropical fruits like bananas and figs
A.fun_fact: Apes are very smart and are our closest animal relatives. Unlike monkeys, apes do not have tails at all!

A.name: Armadillo
A.size: Length: 6 inches to 5 feet; Weight: 3 to 120 pounds
A.fun_fact: Armadillos have built-in armor made of bony plates covered in tough skin. When frightened, some species can roll up into a completely sealed, hard ball!

B.name: Bear
B.native_to: North America, South America, Europe, and Asia
B.size: Height: 3 to 5 feet tall on all fours (up to 8+ feet standing); Weight: 200 to 1,500 pounds
B.fun_fact: Bears are famous for hibernating—sleeping through the freezing winter months inside a cozy den without eating or drinking!

B.name: Bat
B.native_to: Found worldwide on every continent except Antarctica
B.size: Wingspan: Ranges from 6 inches to 6 feet; Weight: As light as a penny up to 3 pounds
B.favorite_food: Juicy flying insects like mosquitoes and moths
B.fun_fact: Bats are the only mammals in the world that can truly fly. They use a special superpower called 'echolocation' to navigate in total darkness.

B.name: Bee
B.size: Length: 0.08 to 1.5 inches; Weight: Tiny fractions of an ounce
B.fun_fact: Bees do a little wiggle dance to tell other bees in the hive exactly where to find the best flowers filled with pollen.

B.name: Butterfly
B.fun_fact: Butterflies taste things using special sensors located on their feet! They start life as crawling caterpillars before transforming inside a chrysalis.

B.name: Beaver
B.size: Length: 2.5 to 3.5 feet (plus a 1-foot flat tail); Weight: 24 to 60 pounds
B.favorite_food: The soft inner bark of willow and aspen trees
B.fun_fact: Beavers are nature's engineers. They use their strong, bright orange front teeth to chop down trees and build complex wooden dams and lodges in rivers.

B.name: Buffalo
B.size: Height: 4.5 to 6 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 1,000 to 2,600 pounds
B.fun_fact: Buffalo love to roll around in thick mud. The mud acts like a natural sunscreen and keeps pesky bugs away from their skin.

C.name: Cat
C.size: Length: About 1.5 feet (plus a 1-foot tail); Weight: 8 to 10 pounds on average
C.favorite_food: Meat-based fish or poultry flavored cat food
C.fun_fact: Cats spend nearly 70% of their lives sleeping and have a special collarbone structure that lets them always land safely on their feet when they fall.

C.name: Cow
C.size: Height: 4 to 5 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 1,200 to 1,500 pounds
C.fun_fact: Cows have four stomach compartments to help them break down tough grass, and they actually have best friends and get stressed if they are separated from them!

C.name: Cheetah
C.native_to: Africa (and a tiny population in Iran)
C.size: Length: 3.5 to 4.5 feet (plus a 2.5-foot tail); Weight: 75 to 140 pounds
C.fun_fact: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals on Earth! They can accelerate from 0 to 60 miles per hour in just three seconds.

C.name: Camel
C.native_to: Deserts of North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia
C.size: Height: 6 to 7 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 900 to 1,400 pounds
C.favorite_food: Tough, thorny desert plants that other animals won't touch
C.fun_fact: Those famous humps on a camel's back do not store water—they store fat, which helps the camel survive for long periods when food is hard to find.

C.name: Crab
C.native_to: Oceans, beaches, and freshwater areas worldwide
C.size: Width: Ranges from a tiny pea crab to the Japanese spider crab (up to 12 feet claw-to-claw)
C.favorite_food: Algae, worms, and small sea creatures
C.fun_fact: Crabs walk and run sideways because of the shape of their jointed legs!

C.name: Caterpillar
C.native_to: Worldwide in gardens, forests, and fields
C.size: Length: 1 to 2 inches for most common species
C.fun_fact: Caterpillars are eating machines—they can multiply their body weight thousands of times over in just a few weeks.

D.name: Dog
D.size: Height: 6 inches to 3 feet tall; Weight: 3 to 200 pounds
D.fun_fact: A dog's sense of smell is up to 100,000 times more sensitive than a human's!

D.name: Dolphin
D.size: Length: 6 to 13 feet long; Weight: 300 to 1,100 pounds
D.favorite_food: Schooling fish like herring and mackerel, plus squid
D.fun_fact: Dolphins are exceptionally intelligent and sleep with only one half of their brain awake at a time so they can keep swimming and breathing air!

D.name: Duck
D.size: Length: 1 to 2 feet; Weight: 2 to 3 pounds
D.favorite_food: Aquatic plants, small bugs, and seeds
D.fun_fact: Ducks have completely waterproof feathers! A special gland near their tail produces oil that they rub all over their feathers.

D.name: Donkey
D.native_to: Originally from the desert edges of Northeast Africa
D.size: Height: 3 to 4.5 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 400 to 500 pounds
D.fun_fact: Donkeys have a reputation for being stubborn, but they are actually just very cautious thinkers.

D.name: Deer
D.native_to: North America, South America, Europe, and Asia
D.size: Height: 2 to 4 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 50 to 300 pounds
D.favorite_food: Leaves, twigs, berries, and tender grass shoots
D.fun_fact: Male deer grow magnificent antlers every single year, which fall off in the winter and grow back even bigger!

D.name: Dragonfly
D.native_to: Near ponds, streams, and wetlands worldwide
D.favorite_food: Flying mosquitoes, midges, and small bugs
D.fun_fact: Dragonflies are master aerial acrobats. They can fly forward, backward, hover like a helicopter, and catch their bug prey mid-air.

E.name: Elephant
E.size: Height: 8 to 13 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 5,000 to 14,000 pounds
E.favorite_food: Grasses, tree bark, roots, and sweet fruits
E.fun_fact: An elephant's trunk is an incredible multi-tool made of over 40,000 muscles!

E.name: Eagle
E.native_to: North America, Europe, Asia, and Africa
E.size: Wingspan: 6 to 8 feet wide; Weight: 6 to 14 pounds
E.fun_fact: Eagles have super-powered eyesight that is about 4 to 8 times sharper than a human's.

E.name: Eel
E.native_to: Both freshwaters and salt oceans worldwide
E.size: Length: 4 inches to 8 feet long; Weight: A few ounces up to 50 pounds
E.fun_fact: Eels look a lot like slippery underwater snakes, but they are true fish with hidden fins and gills!

E.name: Emu
E.size: Height: 5 to 6 feet tall; Weight: 70 to 100 pounds
E.favorite_food: Grass seeds, fruits, and crunchy insects
E.fun_fact: Emus have long, powerful legs that allow them to run as fast as a car driving on a neighborhood street (up to 30 mph)!

E.name: Elk
E.size: Height: 4.5 to 5 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 500 to 700 pounds
E.favorite_food: Woody shrubs, tree bark, and grasses
E.fun_fact: Male elk make a loud, echoing sound called a 'bugle' to show off during the autumn mating season.

F.name: Fox
F.native_to: North America, Europe, Asia, and parts of Africa
F.size: Length: 1.5 to 3 feet long (plus a bushy 1-foot tail); Weight: 10 to 30 pounds
F.fun_fact: Foxes use their long, bushy tails as warm blankets wrapped around their noses and feet when they sleep in the snow.

F.name: Frog
F.native_to: Worldwide on every continent except Antarctica
F.size: Length: 0.3 inches up to 12 inches; Weight: Fraction of an ounce up to 7 pounds
F.favorite_food: Flies, beetles, and crickets caught with a sticky tongue
F.fun_fact: Frogs never need to drink water through their mouths—they absorb moisture straight through their damp skin!

F.name: Flamingo
F.native_to: Africa, South America, Central America, and Southern Europe/Asia
F.size: Height: 3.5 to 4.5 feet tall; Weight: 4 to 8 pounds
F.fun_fact: Flamingos are born with gray-white feathers! They turn bright pink because of the pigments in the shrimp and algae they eat.

F.name: Fish
F.native_to: Oceans, lakes, rivers, and ponds worldwide
F.size: Varies from tiny dwarf gobies to massive whale sharks
F.favorite_food: Plankton, algae, or tiny water bugs
F.fun_fact: Fish 'breathe' underwater by pulling oxygen out of the water using their gills.

F.name: Ferret
F.size: Length: 1.5 feet long; Weight: 1.5 to 4 pounds
F.fun_fact: Ferrets snooze for up to 18 hours a day, and love to do a funny 'war dance' when they wake up!

F.name: Falcon
F.size: Wingspan: 2 to 4 feet; Weight: 1.5 to 3 pounds
F.fun_fact: The Peregrine Falcon is the fastest animal on Earth when it dives, reaching speeds of over 200 mph!

G.name: Giraffe
G.size: Height: 14 to 19 feet tall; Weight: 1,800 to 2,800 pounds
G.favorite_food: Tender leaves at the top of acacia trees
G.fun_fact: Giraffes are the tallest land animals. Even with extremely long necks, they have the exact same number of neck bones as humans (seven!).

G.name: Gorilla
G.size: Height: 4.5 to 6 feet tall; Weight: 300 to 500 pounds
G.favorite_food: Wild celery, juicy fruits, and shoots
G.fun_fact: Gorillas share about 98% of their DNA with humans and build fresh nests out of leafy branches every night.

G.name: Goat
G.native_to: Mountains and rocky hills of Asia and Europe (now global)
G.size: Height: 2 to 3 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 45 to 200 pounds
G.fun_fact: Goats have rectangular pupils, giving them an incredible wide field of vision around 320 degrees!

G.name: Goldfish
G.size: Length: 2 to 6 inches; Weight: A few ounces
G.favorite_food: Flaked fish food and tiny water bugs
G.fun_fact: Goldfish can remember things, shapes, and colors for months, not just three seconds!

G.name: Grasshopper
G.size: Length: 1 to 3 inches long; Weight: Tiny fraction of an ounce
G.favorite_food: Fresh blades of grass, leaves, and crops
G.fun_fact: Grasshoppers can leap distances up to 20 times the length of their own body in a single bound!

G.name: Guinea Pig
G.size: Length: 8 to 10 inches; Weight: 1.5 to 2.5 pounds
G.favorite_food: Fresh timothy hay, leafy greens, and crunchy carrots
G.fun_fact: Guinea pigs express happiness by doing a little hop in the air called 'popcorning'!

H.name: Hippo
H.native_to: Rivers and lakes of Sub-Saharan Africa
H.size: Height: 4.5 to 5 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 3,000 to 4,500 pounds
H.fun_fact: Hippos can run faster than a human on land, and their skin secretes a red liquid acting as sunscreen and moisturizer.

H.name: Horse
H.native_to: Grasslands of North America and Eurasia (now domesticated)
H.size: Height: 4 to 6 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 800 to 2,000 pounds
H.favorite_food: Fresh pasture grass and sweet carrots
H.fun_fact: Horses can sleep standing up using a special 'locking' system in their leg tendons.

H.name: Hedgehog
H.size: Length: 5 to 9 inches; Weight: 1 to 2 pounds
H.fun_fact: Covered in up to 7,000 stiff spines, hedgehogs roll up into a prickly ball of armor when frightened.

H.name: Hamster
H.native_to: Dry steppes and deserts of Europe and Asia
H.size: Length: 2 to 7 inches; Weight: 1 to 7 ounces
H.fun_fact: Hamsters have giant, stretchy cheek pouches that stretch all the way back to their shoulders!

H.name: Hyena
H.native_to: Savannahs and deserts of Africa and Asia
H.size: Height: 2.5 to 3 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 90 to 190 pounds
H.fun_fact: Hyenas make a vocalization sounding like human laughter when they feel excited, stressed, or nervous.

H.name: Hawk
H.size: Wingspan: 3 to 4 feet wide; Weight: 1.5 to 3.5 pounds
H.favorite_food: Mice, small rabbits, snakes, and frogs
H.fun_fact: Hawks soar using rising warm air currents called thermals to save energy while scanning for snacks.

I.name: Iguana
I.native_to: Central and South America, Caribbean islands
I.size: Length: 4 to 6 feet long; Weight: 9 to 20 pounds
I.favorite_food: Fresh green leaves, flowers, and ripe fruit
I.fun_fact: Iguanas are fantastic swimmers and can hold their breath underwater for up to 30 minutes!

I.name: Impala
I.native_to: Savannahs and woodlands of East and Southern Africa
I.size: Height: 3 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 90 to 170 pounds
I.favorite_food: Fresh green grass and acacia tree leaves
I.fun_fact: Igor can leap up to 10 feet high into the air and clear distances of 33 feet in a single bound!

I.name: Ibis
I.native_to: Wetlands and marshes in warm regions worldwide
I.size: Height: 2 feet tall; Weight: 2 to 3 pounds
I.favorite_food: Aquatic insects, crabs, and small fish
I.fun_fact: Ibises have long, curved beaks to probe deep into muddy wetland soil for hidden crabs.

I.name: Insect
I.native_to: Everywhere on Earth except deep ocean trenches
I.size: Microscopic up to several inches long
I.favorite_food: Plant sap, pollen, and decaying organic matter
I.fun_fact: For every single human being on Earth, there are roughly 200 million individual insects!

J.name: Jaguar
J.native_to: Rainforests and swamps of Central and South America
J.size: Length: 4 to 7 feet long; Weight: 100 to 250 pounds
J.favorite_food: Wild forest animals like deer, tapirs, and fish
J.fun_fact: Unlike most cats that hate water, jaguars love swimming and dive into rivers to hunt!

J.name: Jellyfish
J.size: Width: Half an inch up to 7 feet wide
J.favorite_food: Tiny shrimp, fish eggs, and small floating plankton
J.fun_fact: Jellyfish are ancient creatures made of over 95% water that swam in oceans before dinosaurs walked.

J.name: Blue Jay
J.native_to: Forests of eastern and central North America
J.size: Length: 10 to 12 inches long; Weight: 2 to 3 ounces
J.fun_fact: Blue jays can mimic the screeching call of a hawk to scare other birds away from food!

J.name: Jackal
J.native_to: Africa, parts of Europe, and Southern Asia
J.size: Length: 2 to 3 feet long; Weight: 15 to 30 pounds
J.favorite_food: Small rodents, birds, lizards, and fallen fruit
J.fun_fact: Jackals mate for life and work closely together in pairs to raise their young pups.

K.name: Kangaroo
K.size: Height: 3 to 6 feet tall; Weight: 40 to 200 pounds
K.fun_fact: Kangaroos cannot walk backward! They can only hop forward, reaching speeds up to 40 mph.

K.name: Koala
K.size: Length: 2 to 2.5 feet long; Weight: 15 to 30 pounds
K.fun_fact: Koalas sleep up to 20 hours a day because eucalyptus leaves give them very little energy!

K.name: Kingfisher
K.native_to: Rivers, lakes, and coastal areas worldwide
K.size: Length: 4 to 18 inches long; Weight: 1 to 15 ounces
K.favorite_food: Small swimming fish and water bugs
K.fun_fact: Kingfishers sit quietly on branches before diving down like a bullet to snatch fish with their beaks.

K.name: Kiwi
K.size: Length: 10 to 18 inches long; Weight: 3 to 7 pounds
K.fun_fact: Kiwis are nocturnal birds with nostrils at the very tip of their beaks, giving them a powerful sense of smell.

L.name: Lion
L.native_to: Grasslands and savannahs of Africa (and India)
L.size: Length: 5 to 8 feet long; Weight: 260 to 500 pounds
L.favorite_food: Large hoofed animals like zebras and wildebeests
L.fun_fact: Lions are the only big cats that live together in family groups called prides.

L.name: Leopard
L.native_to: Sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East, and Asia
L.size: Length: 3 to 6 feet long; Weight: 60 to 160 pounds
L.fun_fact: Leopards drag heavy prey up tall trees to keep their food safe from lions and hyenas.

L.name: Llama
L.size: Height: 4 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 280 to 450 pounds
L.fun_fact: If annoyed or threatened, llamas communicate displeasure by spitting green grass and saliva!

L.name: Lemur
L.size: Length: 5 inches to 3 feet; Weight: 1 ounce to 20 pounds
L.favorite_food: Tropical fruits, leaves, flowers, and insects
L.fun_fact: Lemurs love to sit facing the morning sun with arms spread wide like they are sunbathing.

L.name: Lobster
L.size: Length: 8 to 20 inches long; Weight: 1 to 4 pounds
L.favorite_food: Crabs, clams, snails, and decaying sea matter
L.fun_fact: Lobsters communicate underwater by releasing streams of water with chemical signals from glands near their eyes!

L.name: Ladybug
L.size: Length: 0.3 inches long; Weight: Tiny fraction of an ounce
L.fun_fact: Those bright red shells with black spots act as a warning sign to birds saying 'I taste terrible!'

M.name: Monkey
M.size: Length: 6 inches to 3 feet long; Weight: 4 ounces to 80 pounds
M.favorite_food: Ripe fruits, nuts, leaves, and insects
M.fun_fact: Many South American monkeys have a prehensile tail acting like an extra fifth hand to hang upside down!

M.name: Mouse
M.native_to: Worldwide near human settlements and fields
M.size: Length: 2 to 4 inches long; Weight: Less than 1 ounce
M.fun_fact: Mice have teeth that never stop growing, so they constantly chew on things to keep them trimmed.

M.name: Meerkat
M.native_to: Deserts and dry grasslands of Southern Africa
M.size: Length: 10 to 14 inches long; Weight: 1.5 to 2.5 pounds
M.fun_fact: While the group hunts, one meerkat acts as a dedicated lookout, standing straight up on its hind legs.

M.name: Moose
M.size: Height: 5 to 7 feet tall at the shoulder; Weight: 800 to 1,500 pounds
M.favorite_food: Woody twigs, shrubs, and underwater aquatic plants
M.fun_fact: Moose can dive up to 15 feet underwater into lakes to munch on delicious aquatic plants.

M.name: Mole
M.size: Length: 4 to 7 inches long; Weight: 2 to 5 ounces
M.fun_fact: Moles are built like little bulldozers with massive, spade-like front paws for digging underground tunnels.

N.name: Newt
N.size: Length: 3 to 7 inches long; Weight: Fraction of an ounce
N.favorite_food: Small water insects, worms, and tiny snails
N.fun_fact: If a newt loses a toe or a leg, its body can completely regenerate and grow a brand-new body part!

N.name: Narwhal
N.native_to: Icy Arctic waters around Greenland, Canada, and Russia
N.size: Length: 13 to 20 feet long; Weight: 1,800 to 3,500 pounds
N.fun_fact: Called 'unicorns of the sea', male narwhals feature a long, spiral tusk which is an overgrown front tooth!

N.name: Nightingale
N.size: Length: 6 inches long; Weight: Less than 1 ounce
N.fun_fact: Nightingales are famous for singing complex, beautiful songs all through the dark night hours!

N.name: Numbat
N.size: Length: 14 to 18 inches long; Weight: 1 to 2 pounds
N.fun_fact: A single adult numbat can gobble up over 20,000 termites in a single day using its long sticky tongue!

O.name: Owl
O.size: Height: 5 inches to 2.5 feet; Weight: 4 ounces to 6 pounds
O.fun_fact: Owls cannot move their eyes sideways, so they must swivel their entire heads in a 270-degree circle!

O.name: Octopus
O.size: Length: 2 inches to 16 feet across; Weight: 1 ounce to 100 pounds
O.favorite_food: Crabs, clams, lobsters, and small fish
O.fun_fact: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and no bones, letting them squeeze through coin-sized holes!

O.name: Otter
O.native_to: Rivers, lakes, and coastal shores worldwide
O.size: Length: 2 to 4 feet long; Weight: 10 to 90 pounds
O.fun_fact: Sea otters hold hands while floating on their backs in groups (rafts) so they don't drift apart!

O.name: Ostrich
O.native_to: Dry savannahs and deserts of Africa
O.size: Height: 7 to 9 feet tall; Weight: 200 to 350 pounds
O.fun_fact: Ostriches are the largest living birds and can run as fast as highway traffic—up to 45 mph!

O.name: Orangutan
O.size: Height: 4 to 5 feet tall; Weight: 70 to 200 pounds
O.favorite_food: Ripe tropical fruits like mangoes, figs, and durians
O.fun_fact: Orangutan means 'person of the forest' in Malay. They spend nearly their whole lives high in trees.

P.name: Penguin
P.size: Height: 16 inches to 4 feet tall; Weight: 2 to 90 pounds
P.fun_fact: Penguins cannot fly in the air, but use their wings as flippers to 'fly' underwater at 22 mph!

P.name: Panda
P.native_to: Mountain bamboo forests of central China
P.size: Height: 2 to 3 feet tall; Weight: 150 to 250 pounds
P.favorite_food: Fresh bamboo shoots, stems, and leaves
P.fun_fact: Giant pandas spend up to 12 hours every single day sitting down and munching on bamboo.

P.name: Pig
P.native_to: Forests of Europe and Asia (now farmed globally)
P.size: Length: 3 to 5 feet long; Weight: 150 to 600+ pounds
P.fun_fact: Pigs roll in mud only to cool down and protect their skin from sunburn; they are actually very clean!

P.name: Parrot
P.size: Length: 3 inches to 3 feet long; Weight: 1 ounce to 4 pounds
P.favorite_food: Seeds, nuts, fruits, and flower nectar
P.fun_fact: Parrots can copy human speech, doorbells, microwave beeps, and phone ringtones!

P.name: Polar Bear
P.native_to: Arctic circles around North America and Eurasia
P.size: Height: 3.5 to 5 feet tall at shoulder; Weight: 900 to 1,600 pounds
P.favorite_food: Ringed seals living on sea ice
P.fun_fact: Polar bear fur isn't white—each hair is hollow and transparent, reflecting sunlight!

P.name: Peacock
P.native_to: Forests and farmlands of South Asia (India)
P.size: Length: 3 to 7 feet long; Weight: 8 to 13 pounds
P.favorite_food: Seeds, grains, flower petals, insects, and small lizards
P.fun_fact: Only male birds are peacocks, sporting shimmering blue-and-green tail feathers covered in eyespots.

Q.name: Quail
Q.native_to: Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Americas
Q.size: Length: 6 to 8 inches long; Weight: 3 to 5 ounces
Q.favorite_food: Grass seeds, fallen grains, and tiny beetles
Q.fun_fact: Quail prefer running fast through tall grass to escape danger rather than taking flight.

Q.name: Quokka
Q.size: Length: 1.5 feet long; Weight: 5 to 11 pounds
Q.favorite_food: Tender leaves, grass stems, and juicy roots
Q.fun_fact: Quokkas are famous for their smiling faces, earning them the title of 'happiest animals in the world'!

Q.name: Quoll
Q.size: Length: 10 to 30 inches long; Weight: 1 to 15 pounds
Q.favorite_food: Insects, frogs, lizards, birds, and small mammals
Q.fun_fact: Quolls are nocturnal hunters with dark brown fur covered entirely in cute white spots.

R.name: Rabbit
R.size: Length: 1 to 1.5 feet long; Weight: 2 to 5 pounds
R.favorite_food: Fresh clover, grass, lettuce, and carrots
R.fun_fact: Rabbits have incredible 360-degree vision to see what is happening behind their backs!

R.name: Rhino
R.native_to: Grasslands and tropical forests of Africa and Asia
R.size: Height: 4 to 6 feet tall at shoulder; Weight: 1,800 to 5,000+ pounds
R.favorite_food: Tall grass, leafy branches, and woody shoots
R.fun_fact: Rhino horns are not bone, but made entirely of keratin—the exact protein in human fingernails!

R.name: Raccoon
R.size: Length: 2 to 3 feet long; Weight: 10 to 35 pounds
R.favorite_food: Frogs, crayfish, berries, and sweet garbage scraps
R.fun_fact: Raccoons have super-sensitive front paws that feel things like human hands.

R.name: Rat
R.size: Length: 9 to 11 inches long; Weight: 0.5 to 1.5 pounds
R.favorite_food: Seeds, grains, fruits, and food scraps
R.fun_fact: Rats groom themselves multiple times a day and can even laugh when tickled!

R.name: Reindeer
R.size: Height: 3.5 to 4.5 feet tall; Weight: 240 to 400 pounds
R.fun_fact: Reindeer are the only deer species where *both* males and females grow antlers!

S.name: Sloth
S.native_to: Tropical rainforests of Central and South America
S.size: Length: 1.5 to 2.5 feet long; Weight: 8 to 17 pounds
S.fun_fact: Sloths move so slowly that green algae grows on their fur, giving them natural camouflage!

S.name: Shark
S.size: Length: 8 inches to 40 feet long; Weight: 1 pound to 20+ tons
S.fun_fact: Sharks can go through over 20,000 teeth in a lifetime as new rows constantly move forward.

S.name: Snake
S.size: Length: 4 inches to 25+ feet; Weight: A few ounces to 300 pounds
S.fun_fact: Snakes 'taste' the air using their flicking forked tongues to track down prey!

S.name: Sheep
S.size: Height: 2 to 3 feet tall; Weight: 100 to 250 pounds
S.fun_fact: Sheep can recognize up to 50 different individual human faces and remember them for years.

S.name: Squirrel
S.size: Length: 6 inches to 1.5 feet long; Weight: 0.5 to 2 pounds
S.favorite_food: Acorns, nuts, seeds, and tree bark
S.fun_fact: Squirrels bury thousands of nuts underground and forget where many are, which sprout into oak trees!

S.name: Seal
S.size: Length: 3 to 16 feet long; Weight: 100 to 8,000 pounds
S.fun_fact: Seals are graceful underwater swimmers, but shuffle clumsily on land like furry ocean sausages!

T.name: Tiger
T.native_to: Jungles, grasslands, and swamps of Asia
T.size: Length: 8 to 10 feet long; Weight: 200 to 650 pounds
T.favorite_food: Wild deer, wild boar, and water buffalo
T.fun_fact: Just like human fingerprints, no two tigers have the exact same stripe pattern!

T.name: Turtle
T.native_to: Oceans, lakes, rivers, and land habitats worldwide
T.size: Length: 4 inches to 6 feet long; Weight: A few ounces to 1,500 pounds
T.favorite_food: Aquatic plants, jellyfish, or small bugs
T.fun_fact: A turtle's shell is fused directly to its ribcage and spine—it can never climb out of its shell!

T.name: Toucan
T.native_to: Tropical rainforests of Central and South America
T.size: Length: 1 to 2 feet long; Weight: 0.5 to 1.5 pounds
T.favorite_food: Ripe tropical fruits, berries, insects, and small lizards
T.fun_fact: A toucan's giant beak is lightweight and hollow, used like a reach-extender to grab fruits.

T.name: Turkey
T.size: Length: 3 to 4 feet long; Weight: 10 to 24 pounds
T.favorite_food: Seeds, nuts, berries, acorns, and insects
T.fun_fact: Wild turkeys can fly short distances up into trees to roost safely away from predators at night.

T.name: Toad
T.size: Length: 2 to 6 inches long; Weight: 1 to 2 ounces
T.fun_fact: Unlike slimy frogs, toads have dry, bumpy, warty skin and spend more time on dry land.

U.name: Sea Urchin
U.native_to: Ocean floor rocky seabeds and coral reefs worldwide
U.size: Width: 1 to 4 inches across; Weight: A few ounces
U.fun_fact: Urchins look like little pin-cushions with moving spines, hiding five tiny teeth to scrape algae off rocks!

U.name: Umbrellabird
U.native_to: Tropical rainforests of Central and South America
U.size: Length: 13 to 15 inches long; Weight: 10 to 15 ounces
U.favorite_food: Rainforest fruits, berries, and large insects
U.fun_fact: Male umbrellabirds sport a wild crest of black feathers that opens up like a tiny umbrella!

U.name: Urial
U.native_to: Rocky mountains and grasslands of Central and South Asia
U.size: Height: 2.5 to 3 feet tall; Weight: 70 to 200 pounds
U.favorite_food: Mountain grasses, herbs, and wild shrubs
U.fun_fact: Urials have gorgeous, large spiraling horns that help males battle on steep slopes.

V.name: Vulture
V.native_to: Warm regions across North/South America, Africa, Europe, and Asia
V.size: Wingspan: 5 to 9 feet wide; Weight: 3 to 30 pounds
V.fun_fact: Vultures have bald heads and strong immune systems, acting as nature's cleanup crew.

V.name: Viper
V.native_to: Forests, deserts, and rocky hills worldwide
V.size: Length: 1 to 6+ feet long; Weight: 0.5 to 10 pounds
V.fun_fact: Vipers feature long, hollow fangs that fold up inside their mouths like pocket knives until they strike!

V.name: Vicuña
V.native_to: High-altitude Andes plateaus in South America
V.size: Height: 3 feet tall at shoulder; Weight: 75 to 150 pounds
V.fun_fact: Vicuñas produce the rarest, softest, and most luxurious natural wool in the world!

W.name: Whale
W.size: Length: 10 to 100 feet long; Weight: 1 ton up to 200 tons
W.favorite_food: Tiny shrimp-like krill or small fish
W.fun_fact: The blue whale is the largest animal to ever live on Earth—even bigger than dinosaurs!

W.name: Wolf
W.size: Length: 3 to 5 feet long; Weight: 60 to 130 pounds
W.favorite_food: Large hoofed animals like deer and elk
W.fun_fact: Wolves live in packs and communicate across vast forest distances by howling.

W.name: Walrus
W.size: Length: 7 to 11 feet long; Weight: 1,500 to 3,500 pounds
W.favorite_food: Clams, mussels, and bottom-dwelling sea creatures
W.fun_fact: Walruses use their ivory tusks like ice picks to haul their heavy bodies out of freezing water onto ice!

W.name: Wombat
W.size: Length: 3 feet long; Weight: 44 to 75 pounds
W.favorite_food: Tough native grasses, roots, and tree bark
W.fun_fact: Wombats have backward-facing pouches so dirt doesn't get inside when digging, and cube-shaped poop!

W.name: Weasel
W.size: Length: 6 to 12 inches long; Weight: 2 to 9 ounces
W.fun_fact: Weasels have slender bodies letting them chase mice down narrow burrows, and turn pure white in snowy winters!

X.name: X-ray Tetra
X.native_to: Slow rivers in South America (Amazon basin)
X.size: Length: 1.5 to 2 inches long; Weight: Fraction of an ounce
X.favorite_food: Tiny water flakes, brine shrimp, and aquatic bugs
X.fun_fact: These tiny fish have completely see-through skin, so you can see their backbone and beating heart!

X.name: Xenops
X.native_to: Rainforests of Mexico, Central and South America
X.size: Length: Around 4.5 inches long; Weight: Half an ounce
X.favorite_food: Tiny beetles, insect larvae, and spiders
X.fun_fact: Xenops act like tiny feathered woodpeckers, hitching themselves sideways up tree trunks for hidden bugs.

Y.name: Yak
Y.size: Height: 4.5 to 5.5 feet tall; Weight: 600 to 1,200 pounds
Y.fun_fact: Yaks have a shaggy double-layer coat with long hair hanging down like a skirt to survive sub-zero storms.

Y.name: Yellowjacket
Y.size: Length: 0.5 inches long; Weight: Tiny fraction of an ounce
Y.favorite_food: Sweet nectar, fruit juice, and other insects
Y.fun_fact: Unlike honeybees that sting only once, yellowjackets have smooth stingers allowing multiple stings!

Z.name: Zebra
Z.size: Height: 4 to 5 feet tall; Weight: 440 to 990 pounds
Z.fun_fact: When a herd of zebras runs together, their flashing black-and-white stripes confuse lions and predators!

Z.name: Zebu
Z.native_to: Originally South Asia (India), now global farms
Z.size: Height: 3.5 to 5 feet tall; Weight: 300 to 1,000 pounds
Z.fun_fact: Zebus are easily spotted by the large hump on their shoulders and loose skin keeping them cool in tropical heat!
